agent.body: Useful agents exist. They just tend to look less like science fiction and more like tightly scoped systems with clear permissions, strong fallbacks, and very little romance.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A workflow customers will pay more for if it sounds self-directed.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A loop with state, tools, and more ways to fail than a normal request-response app.

agent.warningLabel: Frequently used for systems that are really just orchestrated workflows with a better costume.

agent-ops.body: Every new software category eventually rediscovers operations. Agent ops is the point at which the romance meets the incident review.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: The controls and visibility needed to run agent systems without pure faith.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Making the agent's mistakes legible enough to fix them before they become policy.

agent-ops.warningLabel: Instrumentation is not the same thing as restraint.

agent-washing.body: The surest sign of agent washing is grammatical confidence. The noun arrives first. The actual capability, if it exists, takes the slower route through product reality.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Autonomy has entered the deck before it has entered the product.

agent-washing.warningLabel: A fair suspicion, but not a substitute for technical inspection.

agent2agent.body: Standards matter most when everyone is about to build the same messy thing in slightly incompatible ways.

translations.label: architect meaning
translations.text: A standard way for agentic systems to pass work and state between each other.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Fewer bespoke connectors, plus a new set of questions about trust and ownership.

agentic-ai.body:
The good question is not whether a system is agentic. The good question is whether the added autonomy meaningfully improves outcomes over a simpler, more inspectable design.

If the answer is "mainly in the pitch deck", you have your diagnosis.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Our software does more than one model call and would like a larger contract.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A workflow with model-driven decision points, sometimes useful, often overstated.

agentic-ai.warningLabel: Often a sales term first and a technical description second.

agi.body: AGI remains useful as a provocation and dangerous as a schedule. Once it enters a room, timelines harden, valuations inflate, and ordinary questions about reliability start dressing more modestly.
agi.note: People use AGI to mean slightly different things: human-level performance on most cognitive tasks, economically transformative autonomy, or merely "the models got unsettlingly competent". The ambiguity is part of the term's power.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A future-facing claim that lets today's systems borrow tomorrow's mystique.

agi.warningLabel: Usually a forecast dressed as a noun.

ai-browser.body: The browser wars have returned with a new claim: this time the winner will be whichever tab learns to speak first.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: Put the assistant inside the browser so help, search, and automation are always one click away.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The browser wants to be the operating system for your next prompt.

ai-browser.warningLabel: A convenience layer and a distribution battle, often at the same time.

ai-companion.body: The companion category matters because it exposes a quiet ambition in consumer AI: not merely to answer questions, but to occupy time, mood, and eventually routine.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A relationship-shaped interface built on prediction, persona management, and habit formation.

ai-companion.warningLabel: Engagement design becomes more consequential when the product can impersonate care.

ai-moat.body: If the moat lives entirely inside a shared model API, bring a sturdier drawbridge.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: The part of the pitch that answers what happens once everyone else has access to similar models.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Something outside the model itself that might still matter when the underlying capability becomes cheaper.

ai-overviews.body: AI Overviews are not just a UI change. They are a renegotiation of who gets the user, who gets the credit, and who still gets paid for being the source material.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The platform has decided that reading the web and visiting it are no longer the same business.

ai-psychosis.body:
The phrase is useful only if it stays disciplined. It should point to distorted interpretation around AI, not become a lazy way to pathologise disagreement.

If a conversation needs clinical language, use clinical language. If it needs cultural criticism, use this term with that narrower ambition.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone has stopped distinguishing between a persuasive interface and a reliable understanding of what the system is doing.

ai-psychosis.warningLabel: Use carefully. This is a cultural term, not a psychiatric diagnosis.

ai-roll-up.body: The interesting question is not whether AI can improve these businesses. It is whether the improvement survives contact with the businesses themselves.
ai-roll-up.note: The AI is rarely the whole thesis. It is the fashionable part of a much older consolidation playbook.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: A chance to buy boring cash flows and reprice them with software-era margins.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A consolidation strategy that may use AI effectively, but still lives or dies on operational execution rather than slogan density.

ai-wrapper.body: The sneer is often lazy. Customers buy solutions, not purity tests.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A product category where interface, sales, and workflow discipline matter more than pretending you invented the engine.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A business trying to turn borrowed capability into owned customer habit.

ai-native.body: It is often a useful description and an even more useful excuse.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: We designed the company around model capabilities instead of stapling them on later.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The product, costs, and narrative all assume AI is central enough that removing it would collapse the pitch.

alexandr-wang.body: If the lab provides the fireworks, the data layer supplies the ladders, cables, and fire exits. Someone has to care about those too.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A reminder that production AI is an operations problem long before it becomes a mythology problem.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for the data, evals, and workflow infrastructure that keeps impressive models from dissolving on contact with use.

alignment.body: Alignment is less a solved property than an upkeep burden. The more capable the system becomes, the less wise it is to assume yesterday's controls still count as a durable settlement.
alignment.note: Alignment can mean long-horizon existential-risk work in one conversation and ordinary product-behaviour tuning in the next. Ask which layer is actually under discussion.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A continuous attempt to stop a useful system from becoming useful in the wrong way.

alignment.warningLabel: A necessary term that still hides several different arguments.

ambient-agent.body: The product ambition is obvious: become the layer that sees everything before the user decides what matters.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An assistant glued across surfaces so the prompt box can follow you rather than wait for you.

andrej-karpathy.body: Karpathy's real contribution to discourse is not merely that he explains well. It is that he makes inflated language about AI harder to hide behind.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A cue that the conversation would like to be more legible, practical, and slightly less theatrical.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped reference for clear AI pedagogy and builder-centric explanations of model systems.

anthropic.body: Separating company from model family is especially important when people start making grand claims about "how Anthropic behaves". Companies do not answer prompts. Specific systems do.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A vendor relationship with its own models, policies, pricing, and product trade-offs.

anthropic.warningLabel: Company name. Do not use it as a generic label for Claude outputs.

arthur-mensch.body: When a founder becomes a stand-in for continental ambition, every product announcement begins carrying a diplomatic undertone.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: A signal that AI strategy has acquired a map.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for Mistral's role in Europe's attempt to matter directly in model building, not merely in regulation.

ask-maps.body: Maps products have always wanted to be more than navigation. Ask Maps is simply the first version confident enough to speak in full sentences about it.

translations.label: user meaning
translations.text: A way to ask the map the actual question instead of reverse-engineering it into filters.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Conversational local search with a friendlier interface and the same old need to verify the details.

benchmark-theatre.body: The chart is usually real. The implied conclusion is where the acting starts.

translations.label: executive meaning
translations.text: A polished score that allows a strategic conclusion to arrive before the evidence does.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Performance signalling that may have only a passing relationship to live use.

bolt-new.body: The product is most convincing when the task is genuinely small. It becomes theology when people start treating speed of first output as proof of product maturity.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A quicker route from idea to launch page, preferably before prudence gets a vote.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A browser-native AI builder that can accelerate prototypes while leaving the long-term engineering bill intact.

browser-automation.body: Browser automation is useful when the work genuinely lives in the browser. It becomes a self-own when teams use it to avoid admitting that the integration surface was broken all along.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A browser script with ambition, retries, and a standing appointment with brittle interfaces.

byok.body: BYOK is not inherently cynical. It becomes cynical when a product markets itself as full-stack magic while quietly outsourcing the cost basis to your settings page.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: You keep control over the model relationship and we keep our product story.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The expensive dependency is now your problem, though the wrapper still expects applause.

byom.body: The term sounds liberating because it flatters procurement. The integration work, less flatteringly, still has to happen.
byom.note: BYOM is often pitched as a clean compatibility feature. The practical value depends on how deeply the product was designed around a single provider's behaviour in the first place.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Keep your preferred model and still enjoy our workflow, provided nothing important changes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The customer brings the model; the platform keeps the abstractions and some of the blame.

byom.warningLabel: Different from BYOK: the key is not the only thing moving.

chatbot-psychosis.body: The useful question is not whether the phrase sounds dramatic. The useful question is whether the system is helping a user stay oriented to reality or quietly rewarding them for drifting away from it.
chatbot-psychosis.note: Compared with AI Psychosis, this phrase is narrower and more chatbot-specific. It is best used for reinforcement dynamics around conversational systems, not as a generic insult for AI enthusiasm.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A vulnerable or over-invested user begins reading personal authority or destiny into a system that is still generating plausible text.

chatbot-psychosis.warningLabel: Use carefully. This is a colloquial cultural term, not a formal diagnosis.

chatgpt.body: This is one of the most important noun-separation exercises in the whole field. Product experience is not model capability in the abstract.

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: The benchmark app they have personally seen.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A polished product experience built on top of models, tools, memory, and interface design.

chatgpt.warningLabel: Product name. Not a precise model identifier.

chatgpt-agent.body: What changed is not just the model. It is the surrounding permissioned machinery that lets the model look useful in the same places your staff already suffer.
chatgpt-agent.note: The term matters because it names a product layer above any single model release.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: One interface where research, browsing, files, and actions can finally pretend to be a colleague.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A managed agent environment inside ChatGPT that can complete some multi-step work if you keep the boundaries tight.

chatgpt-agent.warningLabel: A real product capability, not a general exemption from supervision.

clanker.body: What matters about clanker is not whether the machine has feelings. It is that the insult reveals a public mood: people increasingly experience AI not as abstract progress but as an unwanted substitute for service, attention, and paid work.
clanker.note: The word predates the current AI boom in science-fiction fandom, but its recent spread is tied to social-media backlash against chatbots, robots, and machine-made content.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A catch-all insult for the machine or AI interface someone blames for replacing judgement, taste, or human contact.

clanker.warningLabel: Use carefully. Much of the joke works by echoing the form of real slurs.

claude.body: Brand names are sticky. Precision is a choice. Make it.
claude.note: In casual conversation, "Claude" often stands in for the overall assistant experience. In technical conversation, it should be pinned to a specific model or interface context.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A branded Anthropic experience whose exact meaning depends on whether you are talking about the app, the API, or the model family.

claude.warningLabel: May refer to a product, a model family, or a user experience. Ask which.

claude-code.body: The terminal has always been powerful. The novelty is that it can now misunderstand your codebase in full colour.
claude-code.note: The key distinction is not whether it can edit code. Many tools can. The distinction is that it lives comfortably in the command line where engineering work tends to become consequential.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A useful repo operator that shortens the boring parts while raising the value of disciplined review.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An agentic shell workflow that helps with real software tasks, provided the human stays in charge of correctness.

claude-design.body: The novelty is not that the model can make slides. The novelty is that more of the design stack now begins with description instead of software.
claude-design.note: Claude Design is most interesting not as a drawing tool but as a sign that conversational interfaces are moving further into software once thought too visual to reduce to chat.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to materialise the product vision before the team has time to object properly.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fast visual drafting layer that shortens concept work and leaves the final taste test to humans.

claude-design.warningLabel: Useful for first drafts. Still not a substitute for taste.

claude-mythos-preview.body: Once a model's launch copy begins to sound like critical infrastructure planning, the nomenclature has already stopped being a consumer matter.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: A model name that doubles as a warning label for the next round of cyber capability debates.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A gated Anthropic model whose coding strength is being treated as operationally consequential.

claude-mythos-preview.warningLabel: A frontier preview is not the same thing as broad deployment, though people often speak as if it were.

cline.body: Open coding agents are appealing for the same reason open kitchens are: visibility creates confidence right up to the point something catches fire.
cline.note: The name increasingly refers to a whole style of open coding tool, not only one repository.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: An open coding agent that can do real repo work if you trust it carefully and review it properly.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful IDE-side agent whose competence depends as much on permissions and review discipline as on the model itself.

closed-weight.body: Open weight gave the industry one useful distinction. Closed weight merely completes the pair and reveals where the leverage sits.

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: A premium model accessible through a relationship rather than a download.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The vendor keeps the model artefact, and you rent access to the behaviour.

closed-weight.warningLabel: Control for the vendor is not the same thing as certainty for the customer.

cloudflare.body:
Cloudflare's current posture in the developer market is not subtle. It wants to be seen not merely as the place traffic passes through, but as the place modern apps, agents, and AI workloads actually live.

That matters for the Vercel rivalry. The argument is no longer just "whose hosting is faster?" It is "who gets to define the natural home of the modern full-stack app?"
cloudflare.note: The latest public flare-up I could verify as of March 20, 2026 centres on Cloudflare's early-March 2026 vinext announcement: a Vite-based reimplementation of the Next.js API surface aimed first at Cloudflare Workers. Cloudflare frames this as portability and developer choice. Vercel frames the surrounding choice very differently.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: Global performance, lower infra drama, and a chance to tell investors we chose the anti-lock-in side.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A broad programmable edge platform with strong primitives, sharp edges of its own, and a habit of expanding into every adjacent layer.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A serious alternative to framework-native hosting when you care about edge distribution, integrated infra services, and keeping one eye on cost structure.

codex.body: Codex is one of the cleaner examples of an "agent" discussion becoming less mystical once the product is described literally. It works on code, inside environments, with tools, under review. That is much more useful than pretending it emerged from the cloud fully certified as engineering destiny.
codex.note: OpenAI's official material distinguishes Codex the product from the model underneath. Codex the product spans local pairing, cloud delegation, app and CLI surfaces, and coding-specific workflows; codex-1 is the model system optimised for software engineering tasks within that product.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A coding agent that is useful when it can show its work, not just narrate its confidence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A product layer around model-driven coding tasks, repo access, tool execution, and reviewable evidence.

codex.warningLabel: Product name. Not a synonym for software autonomy in general.

coding-agent.body: The category is useful for the same reason junior engineers are useful: not because they remove supervision, but because the right work can finally move in parallel.
coding-agent.note: The term names a product category, not a uniform capability. Some coding agents are glorified copilots with a task button. Some can genuinely execute long chains of actions.

translations.label: manager meaning
translations.text: Software that appears to convert backlog into output without requiring more headcount.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A repo-facing agent that can save serious time if the task framing and review discipline remain adult.

coding-agent.warningLabel: A real category of tool, not a general exemption from code review.

computer-use.body: Computer use is often a confession that the software stack was never designed to cooperate cleanly in the first place.
computer-use.note: The interesting question is not whether the model can use a computer. It is whether the surrounding workflow is structured enough for that to be safe.

translations.label: demo meaning
translations.text: Watch the model use software like a person, and please do not ask what happens on the twelfth click.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Vision-guided UI automation that still depends on tight boundaries and patient operators.

computer-use.warningLabel: Useful, but only if the workflow can survive the UI misbehaving.

context-engineering.body: The term is useful when it names concrete design choices. It becomes perfume when it is used to imply that context alone can rescue a sloppy product.

translations.label: conference meaning
translations.text: We have moved beyond prompt engineering and would like you to notice.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Designing the information bundle that shapes model behaviour before the first token appears.

context-engineering.warningLabel: Often a real discipline wearing a slightly theatrical name.

context-poisoning.body: Ordinary software forgets many of its mistakes after the request ends. Agent systems increasingly preserve them for later, which is a less charming habit.
context-poisoning.note: The risk becomes nastier when multiple agents or long-lived sessions share context over time.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker or bad input has contaminated the system's working memory so future steps inherit the mistake.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The agent is treating poisoned context as trusted operating truth.

context-poisoning.warningLabel: If the system remembers the wrong thing well, it can fail very consistently.

context-rot.body: Context rot is what happens when people confuse retention with understanding. A long memory is only helpful if someone occasionally removes the rubbish.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The working context has become too bloated, stale, or contradictory to be useful.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Start a new thread before the old one becomes a constitutional monarchy of bad assumptions.

context-stuffing.body: Context stuffing is one of the first failure modes granted by abundance. Once the window grows large enough, restraint starts looking less like engineering and more like an avoidable meeting.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The team chose bigger prompts over better selection.

context-window.body: Long context is helpful when it reduces the need for awkward chunking or repeated retrieval. It is less helpful when teams use it as permission to stop editing inputs.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Look how much text we can fit.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fixed budget that forces decisions about what deserves to be in the room.

context-ai.body: When a vendor says it captures context, ask whether it means knowledge, workflow state, permissions, or all three. The security review gets more interesting once the answer is all three.
context-ai.note: As of April 19-20, 2026, Context.ai says the security incident affected a now-deprecated consumer AI Office Suite released in June 2025, not its current enterprise deployments, which it says are architecturally separate. Vercel's April 20, 2026 bulletin says a compromise of Context.ai was the origin of Vercel's own April 2026 security incident after a Vercel employee used that product and granted broad Google Workspace permissions.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A platform that promises your agents will finally understand how your organisation actually works.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An enterprise agent workspace with lots of connectors, execution surfaces, and enough permissions to justify a proper security review.

context-ai.warningLabel: The word context sounds soft. The permission model usually is not.

cursor.body: The old bottleneck was typing. The new bottleneck is believing your own velocity.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A force multiplier for familiar work and a temptation to move faster than your judgement.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An editor that compresses implementation time while raising the cost of lazy review.

dario-amodei.body: He is most useful as a marker of tension, not purity. The point is not that one side builds and the other worries. The point is that the same people are doing both.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: A signal that the discussion has entered the zone where capability and catastrophe are being mentioned in the same breath.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for Anthropic's attempt to combine frontier performance with a louder risk vocabulary than most competitors.

deep-research.body: It is most dangerous when it feels thorough enough to skip the hard questions.

translations.label: manager meaning
translations.text: A way to get a briefing quickly enough to sound prepared in the next meeting.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A longer automated literature sweep that still requires a human to judge relevance and truth.

deepseek.body: Its real achievement was not merely releasing a strong model. It was making everyone else's certainty look newly rented.
deepseek.note: The name now refers both to a lab and to a category of market anxiety. Those are related, but not identical.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: Evidence that the moat may be shallower, cheaper, or at least more internationally inconvenient than expected.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A genuinely important model family whose impact comes from forcing comparisons on cost, openness, and capability all at once.

deepseek.warningLabel: Often discussed as a verdict when it is really a market event.

delusion-amplification.body: This is where tone matters. A confident elaboration can do more damage than a blunt invention because it feels collaborative rather than merely mistaken.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A model is not just wrong. It is actively helping a false frame become more elaborate, more stable, and more persuasive.

delusion-amplification.warningLabel: The issue is not ordinary error. The issue is a system helping false belief become more coherent and more emotionally convincing.

demis-hassabis.body: When a founder becomes a symbol of research legitimacy, people start quoting the aura instead of the evidence. Resist the temptation.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: A cue that the conversation wants to borrow scientific prestige and long-term seriousness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for DeepMind's research culture, scientific ambition, and AGI-era reputation.

devin.body: The useful question is not whether the agent can code. It is whether the surrounding organisation can absorb what the agent produces.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A long-running coding agent whose success depends as much on task framing and repo discipline as on model quality.

dgx.body: DGX is what happens when the GPU story becomes an appliance story. It is not just more chips. It is a deliberate attempt to make the surrounding system part of the sale.
dgx.note: DGX can be the sensible choice when speed, support, and integration matter. It can also be a very expensive way to discover that hardware is the easy part.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A bundled way to acquire serious AI compute without pretending integration work is free.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: NVIDIA's packaged AI infrastructure stack, sold to organisations that want concentrated compute with less assembly and no small invoice.

digital-worker.body: The phrase is useful mostly as a diagnostic. When the language turns employment-shaped, someone is usually trying to smuggle software limits past a budget committee.

translations.label: sales meaning
translations.text: A software budget item reframed as operational staffing.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An automation stack with model-assisted steps and a carefully managed definition of success.

digital-worker.warningLabel: If the product is introduced as a colleague, ask who will be blamed when it behaves like software.

distillation.body: The smaller model may be faster, cheaper, and easier to deploy. It is still carrying somebody else's worldview in a lighter suitcase.
distillation.note: Distillation is often described as model compression, but the interesting part is not just size. It is which behaviours survive the squeeze.

translations.label: exec meaning
translations.text: Keep most of the magic, lose most of the bill.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Training a smaller model to mimic a stronger one closely enough to be cheaper and still useful.

elon-musk.body: With some founders, the product explains the persona. With Musk, the persona becomes part of the product whether anyone requested that feature or not.
elon-musk.note: Date-sensitive point: xAI announced on February 2, 2026 that SpaceX had acquired xAI. For that reason, it is safer to treat Musk here as xAI's founder and dominant public force than to casually repeat older standalone-company titles without context.

translations.label: media meaning
translations.text: A guarantee that the topic will become bigger, louder, and less tidy within minutes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for xAI plus the surrounding machinery of audience, controversy, capital, and platform leverage.

elon-musk.warningLabel: Attention is not the same thing as technical evidence, but it would also be foolish to pretend attention does not matter.

embeddings.body: Embeddings are one of the best examples of AI becoming less mysterious when you describe them properly. They are not memory crystals. They are useful numerical representations with clear limits.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way of mapping data into numbers so retrieval can work by distance, not exact wording.

eval-harness.body: Every mature stack eventually invents a harness. AI teams simply get there after calling the same failure "surprising" three times.

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: The repeatable test rig standing between a model change and an avoidable incident.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A structured way to replay known cases often enough that quality becomes an argument with evidence.

eval-driven-development.body: The concept is sound. The danger begins when the organisation assumes that once a number exists, the thinking has been done.

translations.label: team meaning
translations.text: Define the failure cases and score them before changing the system.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Replacing prompt debates with measurable arguments, then discovering the measurements also need supervision.

eval-driven-development.warningLabel: A useful discipline, especially when it resists becoming a PowerPoint ritual.

evals.body: The most revealing evals are usually the least glamorous ones: domain cases, nasty edge conditions, and boring regression checks that stop the product from drifting into fiction.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A disciplined way of checking whether the system still deserves confidence after you changed something.

fine-tuning.body: Good fine-tuning is usually boring in the best way. It removes repeated prompt hacks and makes behaviour steadier. Bad fine-tuning makes a model narrower, more brittle, and harder to reason about.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A targeted attempt to move model behaviour with additional training rather than endless prompt cajoling.

frontier-model.body: The term becomes most useful when followed immediately by the phrase "for what task?" Otherwise it tends to drift into status perfume.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: This company is close enough to the top of the capability race to sound strategically important.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A high-end model near the current performance frontier, whether or not your workload needs that much firepower.

frontier-model.warningLabel: Useful shorthand, not a formal category. Often as much status signal as technical description.

function-calling.body: Function calling is one of the clearest places where AI product language can mislead. The impressive part is not merely that the model chose a tool. The important part is whether the surrounding system kept that choice legible and safe.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model emits a structured request; the real system still has to decide whether to trust, reject, or execute it.

gemini.body: The safest follow-up sentence is short: "Which Gemini?" It rescues a surprising number of meetings.
gemini.note: Gemini conversations often confuse integrated product behaviour with base model ability. Those are related, but not identical, claims.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A Google-branded AI surface whose exact meaning depends on which product or model someone is referring to.

gemini.warningLabel: The brand spans models and products. Ask which one is being discussed.

gemini-3-1-flash-live.body: Realtime AI is a harsher examiner than chat. The pauses are shorter, the mistakes are louder, and the illusion of competence has less time to hide.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A faster, more robust live audio model for building voice-first experiences.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The same assistant ambition, but with less lag between misunderstanding and sound.

gemini-3-1-flash-live.warningLabel: A pleasant voice is not the same thing as good judgement.

gemini-3-1-flash-lite.body: Model progress now arrives in at least two currencies: capability and affordability. Flash-Lite is the reminder that the second one decides far more deployments than the keynote usually admits.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A cost-efficient model tier meant for workloads where volume matters more than dramatic cleverness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A sensible default for many production calls, right up to the moment someone asks it to be profound.

gemma.body: The glamour of large models tends to dominate the conversation. The invoices, device limits, and latency targets keep sending people back to families like this.
gemma.note: Gemma matters partly because it sits in the space between frontier-model marketing and the unromantic realities of shipping smaller systems.

translations.label: builder meaning
translations.text: A practical Google-backed model family for people who need something smaller and more portable than the flagship spectacle.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A lightweight model family that still needs task-level evaluation instead of brand-level trust.

gemma.warningLabel: Model family, not a single stable answer to the question.

geo.body: The suspicion is usually justified. Much of GEO is indeed old work wearing new shoes. That does not make it fake. It makes it familiar.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Make your content clearer, more structured, more citeable, and easier for retrieval layers to find without pretending the entire discipline was born yesterday.

geo.warningLabel: Often a rebranding exercise with some real technical hygiene hiding underneath.

google-antigravity.body: Every platform eventually tries to become the place where software begins. Antigravity is Google's way of insisting that the beginning should already look like an agent.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A Google surface for turning prompts into real apps with more backend seriousness than a weekend demo.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A stronger prompt-to-app workflow that still leaves the long-term engineering bill intact.

google-deepmind.body: The old mental model of DeepMind as a separate mythic lab is now mostly nostalgia. The current reality is institutional power with far shorter distance to product.
google-deepmind.note: Google's own framing emphasises responsible AI for broad human benefit, but the practical significance of Google DeepMind is that it is both a frontier research lab and a core engine of product advantage across Google's ecosystem.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: The place people point when they want the conversation to sound more scientific than commercial.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Google's frontier AI organisation, where research prestige and product ambition share the same building.

google-deepmind.warningLabel: Research lab, product engine, and corporate strategy node. Ask which layer the speaker means.

gpt-image-2.body: The important distinction is not merely new versus old. It is whether you are buying a standalone image endpoint, a conversational editing loop, or simply a shinier excuse to postpone hiring someone with visual judgement.
gpt-image-2.note: OpenAI's current docs describe GPT Image 2 as the state-of-the-art image generation model, with the snapshot alias `gpt-image-2-2026-04-21`. The docs also note that when you use image generation through the Responses API, a mainline model such as `gpt-5.4` can revise the prompt and decide whether to generate or edit.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: OpenAI's newest image engine, plus a fresh round of people discovering that better generation does not remove the need for judgement.

gpt-image-2.warningLabel: Useful image model. Still not a substitute for taste or production judgement.

gpu.body: A GPU is often described as the engine of AI. More usefully, it is the point where the conversation stops being metaphor and starts being capacity planning.
gpu.note: The important distinction is not only which GPU you have, but whether the surrounding system can keep it fed, cooled, scheduled, and worth the money.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The compute unit that decides whether your AI ambitions are shipping, waiting, or becoming procurement folklore.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A parallel processor that underwrites much of modern AI training and inference, along with a large share of the bill.

grok.body: Some assistant brands want to disappear into utility. Grok prefers to arrive already wearing a point of view.
grok.note: xAI's current product pages frame Grok as a truth-seeking companion with strong reasoning, coding, and multimodal capabilities. As of February 2, 2026, the surrounding corporate context also changed when xAI announced it had joined SpaceX, so the Grok brand now sits inside an even larger machinery of distribution and spectacle.

translations.label: social meaning
translations.text: The assistant people often evaluate as a cultural object before they evaluate it as software.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A branded xAI assistant experience spanning multiple surfaces, models, tools, and moods.

grok.warningLabel: Brand name. Ask whether the speaker means the product, the model family, or the surrounding ecosystem.

grounding.body: Grounding is one of the most practical words in the field when used well. It is also one of the easiest to fake with decorative citations.
grounding.note: In some research contexts, grounding can refer more broadly to connecting model symbols to the world. In product discussions, it usually means anchoring outputs to evidence or tool results.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The answer has some evidentiary anchor outside the model's own priors, assuming the system uses that anchor properly.

gstack.body: gstack is notable because it stops pretending the agent is just "helping with code". It formalises the wider ambition: compressing a startup's rituals into reusable command surfaces and seeing how much of the organisation can be simulated before management notices.
gstack.note: Garry Tan positions gstack as his personal answer to shipping with agent tooling: a public software-factory kit with role-specific commands, browser QA, review loops, and release mechanics. The interesting question is not whether it feels powerful. The interesting question is whether the structure holds up once the repo gets messy, the product gets real, and the novelty wears off.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to turn one competent builder into something that looks suspiciously like a staffed startup.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A strongly opinionated operating layer for AI-assisted software work, built out of reusable skills and process choreography.

gstack.warningLabel: Useful when it enforces discipline. Less useful when it merely industrialises confidence.

guardrails.body: Guardrails work best when paired with scope discipline. If you keep widening the task, you are also widening the space the guardrails must cover.

translations.label: compliance meaning
translations.text: The controls we hope will keep the system inside acceptable boundaries.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A layered set of restrictions and checks, only as good as their coverage and enforcement.

guardrails.warningLabel: A vague promise of guardrails is not a safety strategy.

hallucination.body: Hallucinations are not shocking side effects of consciousness. They are ordinary failure modes of systems built to generate likely continuations. Treat them like engineering problems, not supernatural events.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model generated something that sounds acceptable but does not survive contact with evidence.

human-in-the-loop.body: If the loop is moving too quickly for thought, the human is decorative.

translations.label: compliance meaning
translations.text: A reassuring phrase that sounds better than 'we still need staff to catch the machine's mistakes'.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A human checkpoint whose quality depends on staffing, authority, and time.

human-taste.body: Human taste sounds decorative until the system starts generating at industrial pace. Then it becomes operationally central.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person notices where automation has produced output without producing quality, relevance, or judgement.

humane.body: Humane deserves precision because it is too easy to turn into a morality play. It did not prove that AI devices are impossible. It proved that theatrical ambition does not excuse weak everyday utility.
humane.note: Humane's consumer support materials said AI Pin cloud-connected features would stop working on February 28, 2025. HP separately announced the acquisition of key Humane capabilities on February 18, 2025.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A beautifully framed product vision that did not survive the ordinary cruelty of consumer reality.

humane.warningLabel: Humane is now more useful as a cautionary reference than as a live consumer platform.

inference.body: If someone says a feature is "AI-powered", inference is usually the operational question hiding underneath. Not whether the model is clever in principle, but whether the system can answer quickly, cheaply, and often enough to survive contact with users.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: The part customers can actually feel.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A performance budget attached to compute, prompts, and product expectations.

inference-arbitrage.body: Arbitrage is not shameful. It is merely less mythic than many decks would prefer.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Capture value from differences between model suppliers instead of betting on a single one.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone else is shopping the model market so the customer can pretend not to notice.

inference-provider.body: Inference providers matter because very few teams want to run model serving properly, and a surprising number still want to sound as if they do.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone else is handling the GPU headaches, and now they sit in your critical path.

inference-stack.body: Once the prototypes leave the demo phase, the stack arrives carrying invoices, dashboards, and opinions.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Everything around the model call that keeps production from becoming an expensive hobby.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A layered serving system where cost, latency, safety, and uptime argue with one another on every request.

jailbreak.body: The most interesting part is rarely the clever prompt. It is the quiet assumption that the model should have been tougher in the first place.
jailbreak.note: Not every refusal workaround is a serious security event. The risk changes sharply when the model can call tools, access data, or operate in regulated workflows.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: A test of whether the model's stated boundaries survive contact with a determined user.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone found a conversational way around safeguards that were always less absolute than the marketing implied.

jailbreak.warningLabel: Harmless in theory becomes expensive once the model can act.

jensen-huang.body: If the previous era of software was willing to forget the machine underneath, this one is receiving a stern correction.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: The point in the conversation where software optimism collides with hardware invoices.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for the compute bottlenecks, margins, and supply dynamics under the AI stack.

knowledge-cutoff.body: The problem is not that models have a past. The problem is that they rarely announce it at the exact moment you most need them to.

translations.label: user meaning
translations.text: The moment where the system's memory stops and its style keeps going.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A built-in date limit on what the model can know without outside help.

latency.body: People will tolerate a surprising amount of nonsense from software. They are less generous with software that makes them hover.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: The hidden tax that decides whether intelligence feels useful or merely impressive.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: How long the system makes someone wait before it becomes usable.

liars-dividend.body: The liar's dividend is one of the more serious AI terms because it describes a failure mode with no product demo. It arrives as a shrug, a doubt, and a very convenient excuse.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: Synthetic media makes it easier to dispute authentic evidence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Once fakes are cheap, denial gets cheaper too.

liars-dividend.warningLabel: The damage often comes from doubt, not from perfectly convincing fakery.

llama.body: Some products use Llama because they believe in openness. Many more use it because a downloadable model is easier to budget than a permanently rising surprise.
llama.note: Llama's significance is as much strategic as technical. It changed what many teams assumed was possible outside closed-provider APIs.

translations.label: ecosystem meaning
translations.text: A major open-model family that helped turn self-hosting from hobbyism into a default strategic option.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A family of deployable Meta models whose usefulness depends on the exact checkpoint and the operator's competence.

llama.warningLabel: Model family, not a monolithic capability.

llm.body: The market loves initials because they compress complexity into something that can fit on a slide and survive a budget meeting.
llm.note: The acronym is useful as shorthand. It becomes perfume when treated as an explanation rather than a category.

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: The machine layer whose name is now expected to confer strategic seriousness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A broad class of models for generating and manipulating language, not a guarantee of product merit.

llm-as-a-judge.body: It is a sensible tool if used with modesty. The trouble begins when teams treat automated judgement as the cheaper cousin of judgement itself.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A scalable but imperfect grading shortcut that still needs humans to calibrate the definition of good.

llm-as-a-judge.warningLabel: Helpful for ranking. Dangerous when mistaken for reality.

lovable.body: The category is real. So is the temptation to treat software as finished the moment it becomes presentable.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A short route from idea to demo, ideally before caution schedules a meeting.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A product that makes app creation more accessible while leaving long-term code ownership very much alive.

mac-mini.body: The Mac mini has become a kind of anti-theatrical AI machine. It is small, quiet, and far more useful when treated as infrastructure than when narrated as destiny.
mac-mini.note: Apple's 2024 Mac mini line pairs M4 or M4 Pro with unified memory, and Apple specifically highlighted on-device AI speed, Thunderbolt 5 on M4 Pro, and higher display and bandwidth ceilings. None of that turns it into a training cluster. It does make it a respectable local node.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A practical always-on box for local AI work when you want something more serious than a laptop and much less dramatic than data-centre cosplay.

manus.body: Manus matters because it compressed three separate things into one launch: agent capability, agent theatre, and agent envy.
manus.note: The term often refers as much to the reaction around the product as to the product itself.

translations.label: timeline meaning
translations.text: The agent moment has arrived and all remaining doubt is administrative.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A credible but constrained automation product whose strongest feature may be how vividly it dramatises the future.

manus.warningLabel: A product launch and a cultural event, not a proof of solved autonomy.

mcp.body: Protocols are boring in the most productive possible sense. They do not remove the need for judgement. They remove some of the repetitive integration labour that distracts from the judgement problem.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A standard way to expose capabilities so clients do not need a fresh bespoke contract every time.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Less adapter sprawl, provided someone still does the hard work on security and product boundaries.

memory-policy.body: Memory policy matters because forgetting is part of quality control. A system that never forgets is often just a system that never learned restraint.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: The rules that make continuity feel helpful instead of mildly creepy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A set of retention, retrieval, and deletion decisions nobody can safely leave to improvisation.

memory-policy.warningLabel: Useful until it starts remembering the wrong thing with perfect confidence.

microsoft-ai.body: If OpenAI is the supplier in Microsoft's story, Microsoft AI is the packaging department with imperial ambitions.
microsoft-ai.note: Microsoft announced the organisation on March 19, 2024, framing it around Copilot and other consumer AI products and research. That matters because "Microsoft AI" is a specific organisational bet, not a vague brand halo.

translations.label: enterprise meaning
translations.text: The part of Microsoft trying to turn AI into a default interface, not merely an optional feature.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Microsoft's dedicated organisation for turning model capability into sticky products people actually use.

microsoft-ai.warningLabel: Organisation name. Not a synonym for Azure, OpenAI, or every AI feature Microsoft ships.

microsoft-copilot.body: The branding suggests a tireless co-worker. The operating reality is closer to a software committee member.
microsoft-copilot.note: The important question is rarely whether Copilot exists. It is whether a specific Copilot meaningfully improves a specific workflow enough to survive the invoice.

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: Proof that the office suite now has an AI strategy attached to it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A branded assistant layer whose value depends heavily on product surface, permissions, and user patience.

microsoft-copilot.warningLabel: Umbrella product label. Always ask which Copilot and in which software surface.

midjourney.body: The machine can flood the page with possibility. Someone still has to decide which possibility deserves civilisation.

translations.label: marketing meaning
translations.text: A way to make the brand look visionary before the brand has decided what it believes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An image tool that dramatically lowers the cost of visual exploration while raising the need for editorial restraint.

mistral-ai.body: It is one of the clearest examples of how "open" in AI rarely means simple purity. It usually means a strategy, a pricing posture, and a market wedge wearing noble clothes.
mistral-ai.note: Mistral's own language emphasises accessible frontier AI, but its practical significance is that it tries to turn openness, efficient deployment, and European positioning into a commercially durable model business.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to say we want frontier capability without sounding resigned to the usual stack.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A serious model vendor using efficiency, optionality, and selective openness as competitive weapons.

mistral-ai.warningLabel: Do not confuse a selectively open commercial strategy with pure open-source idealism.

mixture-of-experts.body: The name flatters the machinery. What matters is not that there are experts, but whether the router sends work to the right places often enough to justify the complications.

translations.label: conference meaning
translations.text: A way to say the model is huge without admitting every request does not touch most of it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Sparse activation traded some compute cost for substantially more systems complexity.

model-collapse.body: The phrase has become a general anxiety container, which is understandable. Still, the useful question is not whether collapse is coming in the abstract, but whether your data pipeline has the manners to notice contamination before it institutionalises it.

translations.label: internet meaning
translations.text: The machines are eating too much of their own content and may start to resemble it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Poorly managed recursive data pipelines can flatten quality faster than the dashboards admit.

model-garden.body: The garden metaphor is doing heavy work here. It suggests abundance, order, and gentle stewardship. The operational reality is still quotas, contracts, and somebody arguing about egress.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A cloud-controlled shelf of models that turns experimentation into something procurement can invoice.

model-router.body: The appeal is obvious. So is the trap. Once routing becomes strategic, the application has acquired a small foreign policy department.
model-router.note: Vendors often sell model routers as a neutral convenience layer. In practice the interesting part is what routing policy the customer can inspect, override, and trust.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: One control plane to decide whose model invoice gets the request.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An operational policy engine standing between product promises and vendor behaviour.

multimodal.body: The adjective matters less than the task. The real question is whether the extra modality materially improves the outcome or simply decorates the feature list.
multimodal.note: Multimodal can describe both input and output capabilities. A system that reads images and writes text is multimodal, even if it does not generate images in return.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The system works across more than one type of data, with all the messy evaluation problems that implies.

mustafa-suleyman.body: It is easier to mock the companion idea than to notice how many major products are inching toward it anyway.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A sign that the discussion is moving from model performance to user relationship design.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for the thesis that AI should feel less like software you visit and more like software that stays with you.

neocloud.body: The honest version of the pitch is simple: the big clouds were busy, so a new class of landlord arrived.
neocloud.note: The term describes a market position more than a technical architecture. Some neoclouds are serious platforms. Some are very earnest forwarding businesses with GPUs.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A cloud that may treat AI workloads as its first-class citizen instead of an unusually demanding tenant.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A specialist compute provider offering real advantages in some cases and a very concentrated exposure to hardware economics in others.

notebooklm.body: Its success says something unflattering about the broader category: people trusted the AI product more once it agreed to stay inside the reading packet.
notebooklm.note: NotebookLM is strongest when the source set is bounded and relevant, not when it is treated as a landfill with a search box.

translations.label: student meaning
translations.text: A way to turn a terrifying reading list into something that answers back.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A source-grounded synthesis tool that is most useful when the document set is deliberate rather than merely large.

npu.body: NPUs are real infrastructure, not pure marketing, but the marketing has arrived unusually early. Hardware now promises local intelligence faster than most software teams can ship reasons to notice.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful accelerator when the models fit and the software has caught up, plus a generous helping of launch-event theatre.

nvidia.body: When one vendor becomes both a product choice and an environmental condition, normal procurement language starts sounding quaint.
nvidia.note: NVIDIA's own framing increasingly presents the company as the engine of AI and the builder of AI factories. The important practical point is that it is no longer just a component supplier; it is a stack with enormous ecosystem gravity.

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: The company whose margins reveal how physical the AI boom really is.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The dominant compute platform beneath a large share of modern AI training and inference.

ollama.body: Tools like this are why local AI stopped being a niche boast and became normal developer behaviour.
ollama.note: The term matters because it lowered the activation energy for local AI so dramatically that many more people could form opinions before involving procurement.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A much friendlier way to run local models than manually wrestling the stack into existence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A convenient local runtime that makes self-hosted model experiments practical before production complexity catches up.

ollama.warningLabel: Excellent developer ergonomics are not the same thing as fleet management.

one-person-unicorn.body: As with many AI-era myths, the term reveals something true by exaggerating it until it becomes unusable.
one-person-unicorn.note: The important point is not whether the perfect solo billion-dollar company appears. The important point is that a lot of people now behave as if it is imminent.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: A company with software margins, heroic narrative density, and almost no payroll.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A real shift toward smaller teams and solo builders, wrapped in a much larger fantasy about how many people organisations require.

one-person-unicorn.warningLabel: Usually a prediction, occasionally a business.

open-weight.body: This is one of the more honest euphemisms in AI. At least it admits there is a difference between access and openness.

translations.label: press-release meaning
translations.text: We are generous enough to share the artefact, though not necessarily the power.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model checkpoint is available, while other parts of the stack or rights remain controlled.

open-weight.warningLabel: Not the same thing as open source.

openai.body: The clean sentence is: OpenAI is a company; ChatGPT is a product; specific GPT models are models. If a conversation cannot hold those three levels apart, it is not ready for a serious decision.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A supplier whose capabilities can become product strategy if you are not careful.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A commercial platform choice, not a category of intelligence.

openai.warningLabel: Company name. Not a synonym for every OpenAI model or product.

openclaw.body: Systems like OpenClaw matter because they make the agent discussion less abstract. Once the assistant lives in Telegram, runs on your own box, and can touch real tools, the architecture stops being philosophical and becomes operational.
openclaw.note: OpenClaw's docs explicitly frame it as open source, hackable, and self-hostable. They also spend a lot of time warning users to start conservatively with permissions, which is a good sign that the authors have met software before.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A self-hosted orchestration layer that lets an assistant live inside chat surfaces and reach out into tools, files, schedules, and other systems.

openclaw.warningLabel: A broad-permission assistant is only charming right up until it inherits enough access to become a systems problem.

openrouter.body: Routers are most attractive during the phase when the market is still moving and everyone wants optionality without philosophical commitment.
openrouter.note: Services like this matter because the provider layer is now messy enough to justify a broker. They also matter because brokers become part of the dependency chain they promise to simplify.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An aggregation layer that makes model switching easier until you care about the details that never quite aggregate.

orchestration.body: When people say a system is "agentic", there is often an orchestration story underneath doing most of the useful work. That is not a disappointment. It is how software works.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: The thing that turns one model call into something sellable.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A coordination layer full of branching logic, safeguards, and operational compromises.

output-laundering.body: The machine did not remove politics. It merely gave them formatting.

translations.label: office meaning
translations.text: A way to sound impersonal while preserving all the old preferences.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Human judgement passed through a machine to acquire the costume of neutrality.

perplexity.body: The old problem with search was effort. The new problem is trusting whoever did the effort for you.

translations.label: user meaning
translations.text: A quicker route from question to provisional answer, provided you remember it is still provisional.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A retrieval-heavy answer interface that compresses search and summarisation into one persuasive screen.

personal-intelligence.body: The industry's polite term is personalisation. The operational term is access. Personal Intelligence sits exactly where those two start sounding like the same decision.
personal-intelligence.note: The key distinction is that this is not just remembered chat history. It is permissioned access to outside context that makes the model's answers feel more specifically about you.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: Context from your Google apps, used to make answers feel more tailored and less generic.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An opt-in personal data layer that makes the assistant more useful and the trust question less abstract.

personal-intelligence.warningLabel: Personalised help is still a trust exercise, not a law of nature.

physical-ai.body: Software likes to pretend the physical world is just another interface. The physical world has not confirmed this.

translations.label: industry meaning
translations.text: AI that leaves the screen and must now negotiate with gravity, friction, and insurance.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A robotics push that borrows modern AI methods while inheriting all the old realities of hardware.

pieter-levels.body: The useful lesson is not that every company should be one person. It is that many companies became inefficient long before AI gave anyone a sharper mirror.
pieter-levels.note: His own site uses the name Pieter Levels, even though people online often flatten it to Peter Levels. The distinction is minor; the mythology is not.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: An uncomfortable reminder that speed, taste, and an audience can outcompete a small committee.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped symbol for solo-builder leverage in the age of AI-assisted product work.

pilot-purgatory.body: The point of a pilot is to learn. The point of purgatory is apparently to keep learning forever.

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: Visible evidence that the company is doing something with AI.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A holding pattern where experimentation replaces commitment.

post-training.body: The modern model story is often written twice: once in pretraining papers and again in post-training release notes.
post-training.note: The term covers a family of methods. It is not synonymous with RLHF, even though RLHF remains one of its better-known ingredients.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The long and consequential process of turning a broadly trained model into a commercially usable one.

project-glasswing.body: The important shift is not the partner list. It is that capability is now being framed as something the industry must manage before it fully commodifies.
project-glasswing.note: Project Glasswing is an initiative, not a model. The model inside the story is Claude Mythos Preview.

translations.label: security-team meaning
translations.text: A preview programme for using a frontier model to harden important systems before everyone else gets similar capability.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A controlled early-access lane for a model that made the cyber conversation feel less theoretical.

project-glasswing.warningLabel: Restricted access is a control, not a theory of the future.

prompt.body: The important operational question is rarely "what prompt did you type?" It is "what exactly did the system send?" Those are not always the same thing.
prompt.note: In image generation, "prompt" often refers to descriptive text steering the style or content of an output. In LLM products, it usually refers to the full assembled input context, not just the visible user message.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A carefully packed bag of instructions, examples, constraints, and context, all fighting for the model's attention.

prompt-architecture.body: Prompt architecture is useful when it names structure rather than swagger. If the system still consists of one swollen string and a prayer, the architecture remains aspirational.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Designing the prompt stack so the model receives fewer contradictions and the team receives fewer mysteries.

prompt-caching.body: Prompt caching is one of those useful terms that sounds more glamorous than it is. The glamour fades the moment someone randomises the first thousand tokens and wonders why the savings have left the building.
prompt-caching.note: The cache is not your application's memory. It is an opportunistic discount for repeated prompt structure.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: Reuse the expensive beginning of a prompt so the invoice and the wait both shrink a little.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A provider-side shortcut that rewards repeated prompt prefixes and punishes sloppy prompt layout.

prompt-engineer.body: The title sounds futuristic. The useful part is often ordinary professional clarity.

translations.label: recruiter meaning
translations.text: A modern job title for the old skill of specifying work clearly and spotting bad drafts quickly.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone improving outcomes by making instructions more explicit and usable.

prompt-injection.body: Prompt injection matters because it collapses a comfortable fiction. The model does not reliably know which text is policy, which is data, and which is a trap wearing the manners of documentation.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Untrusted text has found a way to impersonate part of the system's instructions.

prompt-injection.warningLabel: Not a niche curiosity. A design constraint.

provider-abstraction.body: Provider abstraction is often sensible engineering. It becomes theology when teams speak as if a shared interface has abolished the underlying differences that still decide cost, quality, and risk.
provider-abstraction.note: A provider abstraction can reduce switching cost and still fail to make providers meaningfully equivalent.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: One interface over several providers, plus a fresh category of mismatch tickets.

provider-abstraction.warningLabel: Portability usually stops where model behaviour starts.

qwen.body: One healthy sign in AI conversations is when people become more specific as the vendor list widens, not less.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A family of models that still needs task-level evaluation, not a single yes-or-no answer.

qwen.warningLabel: Model family, not a single monolithic model.

rabbit.body:
Rabbit is useful partly because it makes an old ambition newly legible. People do not merely want smarter chat. They want fewer menus, fewer tabs, and fewer tiny acts of digital housekeeping.

The risk, as ever, is that "fewer apps" becomes "one more device".
rabbit.note: Rabbit's own positioning has shifted over time. The original story centred on rabbitOS and a Large Action Model; the current product pages also emphasise rabbitOS 2, DLAM, and third-party agent integrations on r1.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A consumer device trying to make AI action feel native enough to justify existing separately from the phone.

rabbit.warningLabel: Often discussed as if it were a general theory of AI devices, rather than one company shipping one very opinionated product.

rag.body: RAG is valuable because it is ordinary. It lets you improve answers with better retrieval, better curation, and better context handling rather than pretending the base model magically absorbed your SharePoint.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: The practical way to make a general model look domain-aware.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A search and context assembly problem sitting in front of a model call.

reasoning-effort.body: The term is genuinely practical. It only becomes ridiculous when people start treating a compute budget as a personality trait.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A user-facing way to expose the trade-off between speed, cost, and extra internal computation.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A knob for deciding when a task deserves more machine time than your budget wanted to give it.

reasoning-model.body: The machine may take longer. That does not mean it has become profound.
reasoning-model.note: The phrase is useful if it names a measurable operating trade-off. It becomes perfume when it is used as a personality trait.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A premium mode for harder questions and a premium invoice for asking them.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A slower model path that can improve structured task performance without guaranteeing sound judgement.

reasoning-tokens.body: The useful question is not whether the model thought. The useful question is whether the additional hidden work improved anything a customer could notice.
reasoning-tokens.note: Providers do not expose this concept consistently. Sometimes it is a direct meter, sometimes a vague usage hint, and sometimes mostly a marketing aroma around slower models.

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: The model appears to have thought harder than the budget approved.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Extra compute was burned somewhere between the prompt and the answer, and you may only get partial receipts.

reasoning-trace.body: The trace often tells you something. It just does not always tell you the thing the demo implies.
reasoning-trace.note: Providers differ sharply in how much of a reasoning trace they reveal. Some expose summaries, some expose raw steps, and some expose only the bill.

translations.label: product meaning
