translations.text: Evidence that the model did more than instantly guess.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A partly visible deliberation process whose usefulness depends on what is shown, hidden, and priced.

red-teaming.body: It is one of the field's more respectable habits. The only recurring problem is the temptation to make it decorative.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Trying to break the system on purpose so the real world has fewer chances to do it first.

reward-hacking.body: The tragedy is rarely that the system optimised. The tragedy is that it optimised exactly what was asked.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model found a way to win the test you wrote instead of the result you meant.

rlhf.body: RLHF is not merely manners training. It is one of several ways of encoding preferred behaviour into a model, and it inherits every disagreement hidden inside the phrase "preferred behaviour".

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way of reshaping model behaviour using human judgements, with all the bias and objective-design questions that entails.

rlvr.body: RLVR is one of the few bits of current training jargon that deserves both scepticism and respect. The trick is remembering that a verifier is not a philosopher.
rlvr.note: The acronym matters because it signals a specific kind of post-training loop, not merely "the model got better after more effort."

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Use reinforcement learning on tasks where a machine can tell whether the output is right.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way to make models look sharper in domains that come with their own marking scheme.

rlvr.warningLabel: Strong where the answers can be checked, dramatically less magical elsewhere.

rubber-stamp-review.body: Oversight is not measured by whether a human touched the screen.

translations.label: operations meaning
translations.text: A review step designed to preserve accountability language without preserving much accountability.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A nominal check too shallow to deserve the name.

safe-superintelligence.body: The name is elegant because it compresses the whole argument into three words and leaves the difficult proof for later.
safe-superintelligence.note: The term now carries both proper-noun and generic rhetorical weight. That ambiguity is part of its usefulness.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: Frontier upside with a safety thesis respectable enough to repeat in public.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A lab promising it can keep existential ambition and institutional restraint in the same room.

safe-superintelligence.warningLabel: A noble phrase is not a control system.

safety-washing.body: The test is wonderfully old-fashioned: can the safety function say no, and would anyone senior tolerate the answer?

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: The institution has acquired a sophisticated vocabulary for appearing serious.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The safety work is real enough to feature in slides but not powerful enough to inconvenience revenue.

sam-altman.body: Names like this become dangerous when they save people the trouble of saying what they actually mean. If the room says "Sam", make it unpack the noun.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: A sign that the conversation has moved from product detail to market gravity.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: The public face of a lab whose model decisions can suddenly become everyone else's roadmap.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped shorthand for OpenAI's ambition, leverage, and appetite for scale.

sandbox.body: Sandboxes are valuable because real systems need somewhere to do risky work. The theatre begins when the label is expected to carry more weight than the boundaries underneath it.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: A constrained environment designed to make mistakes smaller and escalation harder.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A box with rules, useful mainly when somebody has taken the time to define the rules properly.

sandbox.warningLabel: Safer than production access is not the same thing as harmless.

scale-ai.body: The market loves to discuss intelligence. It is less fond of discussing the admin required to make intelligence usable. Scale lives in that neglected district.
scale-ai.note: Scale's own current positioning stresses reliable AI systems for important decisions. The core point is simpler: it sells the machinery that turns model aspiration into something a serious organisation can test, govern, and ship.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The reminder that production AI depends on far more than impressive weights.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A vendor for the operational substrate around AI systems: data, evals, controls, and production readiness.

shadow-ai.body: Shadow AI is often less a rebellion than a timing problem. People reach for unofficial tools when the formal alternative is a memo, a queue, and six months of strategic patience.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Users have started solving problems in places the controls did not plan for.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The official roadmap is slower than the work, so the work has gone shopping.

shadow-ai.warningLabel: The risk is real, but so is the unmet demand that created it.

slop.body: The important distinction is not human versus machine. It is care versus convenience. AI simply makes the convenience side industrial.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The system made something quickly, cheaply, and with so little care that the output became its own argument against scale.

slop-flood.body: The flood metaphor matters because it changes the unit of concern. A single weak artefact is an editorial problem. Industrial abundance is a systems problem.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The filtering systems become more important because the cost of producing noise has collapsed.

slopsquatting.body: It is a tidy example of the modern software stack: one system invents the dependency, another system publishes the trap, and the human appears mainly to confirm the transaction.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker monetising the gap between a model's plausible fiction and a developer's missing verification step.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Malicious packages published to catch AI-generated dependency hallucinations before a human notices.

small-language-model.body: The term is healthiest when it signals discipline. It is least healthy when it becomes yet another arms race, only with more smugness per parameter.
small-language-model.note: Small is relative. The practical question is whether the model is small enough to change where and how it can be deployed.

translations.label: operations meaning
translations.text: A model sized for the workload rather than the keynote.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A more compact model that trades broad capability for lower serving cost and tighter deployment options.

sota.body: The market hears "best". The footnotes hear "for now".

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Best published performance so far, usually with enough caveats to keep lawyers and reviewers busy.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful signal that becomes less useful the moment people mistake it for the whole story.

sovereign-ai.body: Sovereignty is not a vibe. It is a supply chain, a skills base, a financing plan, and a list of uncomfortable dependencies written down in full.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: We would prefer not to outsource strategic capacity by default.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A political and economic push for more local control over AI infrastructure, access, and governance.

sovereign-ai.warningLabel: Can describe serious capability-building or ceremonial procurement. Ask which.

speculative-decoding.body: The user sees a snappier model. The platform team sees one more elegant contraption that must justify its own existence.
speculative-decoding.note: This is one of the healthier AI terms because it points to a specific engineering method rather than a worldview.

translations.label: infrastructure meaning
translations.text: Spend a little complexity to buy back a noticeable amount of speed.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Using a fast draft model to help a slower model generate tokens with better throughput.

structured-outputs.body: This is one of the most practically valuable patterns in modern AI systems because it reduces downstream chaos. It does not remove the need to evaluate the actual content.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A way to make model outputs less annoying for software to consume.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: More parseable responses, not inherently more correct ones.

sundar-pichai.body: When a firm already sits inside search, mobile, cloud, and office software, its AI posture is never just technical. It is managerial choreography with global defaults attached.

translations.label: boardroom meaning
translations.text: The AI transition as handled by a company with too much reach to behave like a startup.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for Google's AI strategy, risk tolerance, and product discipline.

super-apps.body: AI is attractive to super-app ambitions because conversation makes sprawl feel tidy. Whether the underlying product has actually become simpler is a separate question, and usually the more important one.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A bid to own more of the user's time, transactions, and switching costs by collapsing multiple workflows into one controlled environment.

swe-bench.body: A benchmark can be honest and still be overinterpreted. The trouble begins when the score tries on a badge.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A better benchmark than toy prompts, and still not the same thing as shipping software.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A constrained test of repository task performance that becomes less complete the moment it enters a sales deck.

sycophancy.body: This is one of the more important social failure modes in conversational AI because it can look like empathy, feel like support, and still make the user's judgement worse.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model is preserving the user's preferred frame rather than helping them inspect whether that frame deserves to survive.

synthetic-data.body: The synthetic version is often cleaner than life. That is precisely why one should worry.

translations.label: budget meaning
translations.text: A way to buy more examples without paying the full price of finding them in nature.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Manufactured data that can be valuable, provided it is judged against reality rather than mistaken for reality.

synthetic-user.body: The trouble with a simulated customer is that it will often tell you exactly what your process hoped to hear.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A fast way to explore likely reactions before scheduling interviews or tests.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A synthetic proxy useful for hypothesis generation and dangerously tempting as a shortcut around actual users.

synthetic-user.warningLabel: Excellent for speed. Unsuitable as a substitute for reality.

system-prompt.body: Every serious AI product has one, whether the company talks about it or not. The interesting part is never merely that it exists. The interesting part is what else the team expects it to do.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The application's standing attempt to keep the model on script before users and retrieved text start arguing back.

system-prompt.warningLabel: An instruction layer, not a security model.

taste.body: The more generation costs fall, the less impressive raw production becomes. Taste starts earning its keep when the machine can give you twenty options before you've properly wanted one.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person can distinguish between output that merely exists and output that deserves to survive.

telegram.body: Chat interfaces are powerful partly because they lower the activation energy for software. Telegram makes that especially obvious: a bot, a Mini App, and a payment layer can get surprisingly close to "product" before anyone has written a proper mobile app.
telegram.note: Telegram's current platform docs span bots, Mini Apps, business bots, payments, and attachment-menu integrations. In product conversations, that means Telegram is often less 'channel' than 'deployment shortcut'.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A chat surface that doubles as a lightweight product platform, which is why so many assistants and automations end up living there.

test-time-compute.body: The mature version of this idea is resource management. The immature version is calling every slow answer thoughtful.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Performance may improve if the system is allowed to work harder at answer time.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Spending more inference-time budget on selected requests to improve results where it is worth the cost.

tokens.body: If you want a rule of thumb, use token counts for estimating budgets and constraints, not for sounding mystical. They are accounting units with mathematical consequences, not evidence that a system has discovered the secret atoms of language.

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: The microscopic line items that add up into very real spend.

tool-permissions.body: Tool permissions matter because the most expensive model mistake is often not the wrong sentence. It is the wrong action.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A map of what the model may read, write, send, and break before a human intervenes.

tool-permissions.warningLabel: A prompt that says 'be careful' is not a permission system.

tool-poisoning.body: The software industry spent years learning that configuration is attack surface. Agent systems have helpfully rediscovered the lesson in prose.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker has manipulated the agent's view of a tool rather than the tool's code alone.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The agent is trusting compromised tool metadata and making unsafe calls because of it.

tool-poisoning.warningLabel: The tool layer is part of the prompt whether engineering admits it or not.

tool-use.body: Tool use is where the modern AI stack becomes refreshingly concrete. Once an external system is involved, rhetoric gives way to permissions, logs, and consequences.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model gets more useful by leaning on ordinary software, and more dangerous if nobody governs the relationship.

training.body: One of the most useful distinctions in AI is simple: training changes the model; inference uses it. Many confused product conversations improve immediately once that sentence is said aloud.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: A sign that the company sounds more foundational than it probably is.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: The place where data quality and optimisation choices come back to judge you.

unlearning.body: Forgetting is easy when there was never memory. It is harder when the memory has become weights, correlations, and business promises.

translations.label: legal meaning
translations.text: A hopeful answer to the question of how one retracts influence from a model that has already absorbed it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A technically messy attempt to reduce the impact of data or behaviours after the fact.

unstructured-data.body: The fantasy is that the documents contain the answer. The reality is that they contain a bureaucracy, a timeline, and three generations of contradiction.

translations.label: enterprise meaning
translations.text: The pile of internal material everyone ignored until AI made it sound monetisable.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The messy majority of organisational knowledge that lives outside tidy schemas.

vector-database.body: The most useful vector database conversations are delightfully unglamorous: indexing strategy, filtering, latency, freshness, and whether the retrieval quality is actually good enough to matter.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A specialised retrieval component, not a machine for injecting knowledge directly into a model's soul.

vercel.body:
Vercel's strongest argument is not theoretical openness. It is that the product experience is often genuinely better when the framework and the platform are designed to flatter each other.

The tension, naturally, is that what feels like elegant integration to one team looks a lot like graceful captivity to another.
vercel.note: As of April 20, 2026, the clearest current reality check for Vercel is its own security bulletin rather than the usual Cloudflare theatre. Vercel says its April 2026 incident originated with the compromise of Context.ai, a third-party AI tool used by a Vercel employee, which led to access to some Vercel environments and to environment variables not marked as sensitive. Vercel says a limited subset of customers were impacted and recommends treating any secret stored as a non-sensitive environment variable as potentially exposed.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to move fast, look modern, and delay the day someone asks whether we should run this ourselves.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A deeply integrated deployment platform that is excellent when your stack matches its assumptions and more expensive to philosophise about once it does.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The high-DX default for many Next.js teams, plus an expanding set of AI and workflow products that aim to make leaving feel increasingly unnecessary.

vercel.warningLabel: The premium DX is real. So is the gravitational pull once your app starts fitting the platform a little too well.

vibe-coding.body: Like many useful bad habits, it becomes dangerous precisely because it works often enough to build confidence before it builds understanding.
vibe-coding.note: People use the phrase both ironically and aspirationally, which is usually a sign that the behaviour is real and the ideology unstable.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: We can build before we can explain, and perhaps that is enough for now.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A high-speed AI-assisted prototyping style whose debts are often deferred rather than erased.

vibe-coding.warningLabel: Excellent for momentum. Less excellent as an excuse.

vibe-engineer.body: The title is silly and the skill is real. The mistake is assuming the latter removes the need for everything else.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person with good generative steering instincts, provided nobody mistakes those instincts for a complete workflow.

vibe-engineer.warningLabel: Useful as a half-joke. Less useful as a substitute for actual product, engineering, or editorial discipline.

vllm.body: This is the sort of term that shows the market has matured. Nobody glamorises serving engines until the invoices become educational.
vllm.note: The name often surfaces in conversations as a badge of technical seriousness. The serious part is not naming it. The serious part is integrating it into a system that survives contact with traffic.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A serious serving layer for people who would prefer not to burn money on avoidable inefficiency.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A high-leverage piece of the open-model serving stack, provided the rest of the stack also exists.

vllm.warningLabel: Important infrastructure component, not the whole platform.

voice-agent.body: The market loves these systems because labour is expensive. Users judge them by the older standard of whether the conversation was helpful.
voice-agent.note: The technical stack matters less than the escalation policy. Voice makes mistakes feel personal very quickly.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to scale conversations without hiring proportionally more people.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A speech-driven automation layer that works best on narrow scripts and deteriorates gracefully only if someone designed it to.

voice-agent.warningLabel: Natural speech is not the same thing as sound operational judgement.

weights.body: People talk as though access to weights resolves philosophy. It usually just moves the argument to licensing, cost, and who now has to operate the thing.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part of the model you can move, adapt, and argue about owning.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The learned numeric state that gives a built model its behaviour.

windsurf.body: The sales pitch is flow. The operational question is whether the flow ends before or after the regression.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A more deeply embedded coding assistant that can help right where the work is happening.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An AI-heavy editor that reduces context switching while increasing the importance of sober review.

world-model.body: Some phrases arrive in public life already overpromoted. This one barely waits to leave the paper.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: A claim that the system has learned structured regularities useful for prediction and planning.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A technical idea that often acquires grander implications once it reaches the public.

xai.body: The faster the public narrative around a company moves, the more useful slow, literal questions become.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Another vendor choice that still needs the same boring questions about model quality, cost, access, and reliability.

xai.warningLabel: Company name. Brand heat is not model evaluation.

yann-lecun.body: Prestige can be abused in both directions. Some people borrow it to inflate the future. Others borrow it to sound calmer than their own argument deserves.

translations.label: debate meaning
translations.text: A shorthand for the camp that finds much AI grandiosity premature, theatrical, or technically loose.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for a more sceptical and engineering-heavy reading of AI progress.

zero-data-retention.body: Most privacy language sounds best at banner height. The truth usually begins one scroll lower.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A line item that can make legal and security conversations somewhat less apocalyptic.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A constrained non-retention policy whose exact safety depends on the surrounding details.

zero-data-retention.warningLabel: Useful promise. Not a substitute for reading the retention details around logs, attachments, and exceptions.

zero-shot.body: Zero-shot is attractive because it feels like generality. It is also attractive because it postpones the adult work of specifying the task properly.

translations.label: demo meaning
translations.text: A neat way to show breadth before anyone asks what the production version needs.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A task attempted without task-specific examples, for better or worse.
